text: Dr. Sunil Sharma performs a robotic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. The ports are in place, and the surgeon opens the pars flaccida, providing a clear view of the left gastric vessel and the underside of the stomach. A vessel sealer is used to dissect the mesentery near the left gastric vessel. A 45mm intelligent stapler is inserted to create a gastric pouch, with the first staple fired. A bougie is inserted, and fat over the greater curvature is dissected. The second staple is fired vertically, forming a small pouch, followed by a third staple firing to nearly complete the gastric pouch.

A drain is placed around the small bowel at approximately 150 centimeters, followed by splitting the bowel to create a Roux limb. The surgeon passes a non-absorbable suture to form the posterior layer of the gastrojejunostomy using a continuous V-lock suture. The video shows no tension between the small bowel and the gastric pouch. An enterotomy is made on both the gastric pouch and the Roux limb, and a stapler is introduced.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy using absorbable 2-0 V-lock sutures, starting at two ends and tying at the center. A fourth layer of non-absorbable V-lock suture is applied to bury the staple line, minimizing leakage risk. The robotic sewing is precise and hemostatic, completing the gastro-jejunostomy.

The surgeon measures the length of the Roux limb and creates an enterotomy at 150 centimeters using a hook. The biliary enteric limb is also prepared with an enterotomy. An articulating 45mm stapler is used to establish a side-to-side connection between the two limbs, followed by closure of the enterotomy with absorbable 2-0 V-lock sutures. The robotic arm assists in aligning tissues, ensuring good hemostasis and visualization. An endoscope is passed to check the anastomosis using air insufflation.
